text: PRESENT. Chair Melanie Cutress (MC) Helen Herbert (HH) Martin Gapper (MG) Andy Pearce
MINUTES OF THE LAST MEETING. were signed as a true record of that meeting.
QUESTIONS FROM THE FLOOR. To be dealt with as the meeting progresses.
Football matches had restarted. Fans could attend home matches from Dec 7th but only •
Football car passes would be distributed by then. •
ESCC had completed cutting roadside verges as agreed with us. We had paid for 4 extra •
cuts. All councillors agreed to 4 extra cuts for 2021 cost £168.
The pond grass area had only been cut once in 2020 which was in August. MC would like •
to use some of the new CIL award to pay for a general tidy up around the pond of self
seeded vegetation. This vegetation was becoming substantial bushes and small trees and
The cycle barrier on Middle Street had been repaired. •
The road sign for Park Street had been put up again having been knocked down by a car. •
Two of the three empty properties had now been renovated.
80 Mill St vacated Nov 2016 now occupied. •
81 Mill St vacated June 2018 still empty •
71 Park St vacated Sept 2017 was still derelict but MG reported that it had just been gutted •
and work was expected to begin.
Old Granary conversion. Mr and Mrs MacRae SDNP/20/02491/FUL. Application refused. •
Court Farm Barns .Mr Huxham. Change of use to a dance studio and art gallery. •
Park Farm Shop. Matthew Lenihan non illuminated signs.SDNP/20/03405/ADV. •
New house in the garden of Rose Cottage. East Street. SDNP/20/04592/FUL. Comments to •
be submitted by Dec 17th. All councillors agreed to an objection. Overdevelopment of a
small area and zinc roof and rooflights not in keeping with the conservation area
All councillors agreed to precept again for £500. •
We had a 2020 CIL community infrastructure levy award of £1876. This came from the •
building of holiday cottages in Falmer at Court Farm. It had to be spent within 5 years and
details on what had been spent had to be announced annually. CIL awards can be spent on
infrastructure maintenance and Jessica Riches from SDNP planning had said that it could
be spent on Falmer pond maintenance subject to landowner’s consent.
AD suggested we made a donation to citizens advice who do such valuable community
Denis Plowman had repaired the notice board that was situated on the wall outside the
Lewes Local Plan was nearly 5 years old and housing targets had been met. Until the plan
was revised then housing numbers would be governed by housing need assessed at 800
per year. This took no account of land supply identified already as suitable. It could be a
period of land grab for developers before the local plan was revised.
BHCC were holding zoom consultations on the WEP …Whole Estate Plan…. which was the •
land the city owned that surrounded the city, mainly acquired after the war from the
Chichester estate and was now in the SDNP. The idea was to set a vision for the estates
future. It would be a non statutory plan providing aspirations for the future. It was 12,800
acres of which 78% was farmland. Consultation would continue in the spring.
Chairman’s fund. MC asked councillors to approve a fund to provide things like gifts for •
volunteers. In particular Veronica Lenihan who did our accounts and Pat Asquith who
weekly checked our rubbish bins, removed rubbish and put in new bin liners. All agreed this
was a good idea and we should give a bottle of wine at Christmas and take them out for
